name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
فَلَمَّا رَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِيهِمْ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مُنِعَ مِنَّا ٱلْكَيْلُ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَآ أَخَانَا نَكْتَلْ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ (٦٣)
```

```english
So when they returned to their father, they said, ‘Father, the measure has been withheld from us, so let our brother go with us so that we may obtain the measure, and we will indeed take [good] care of him.’ (63)
```

```arabic
قَالَ هَلْ ءَامَنُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا كَمَآ أَمِنتُكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَخِيهِ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ فَٱللَّهُ خَيْرٌ حَـٰفِظًا ۖ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (٦٤)
```

```english
He said, ‘Should I not trust you with him just as I trusted you with his brother before? Yet Allah is the best of protectors, and He is the most merciful of merciful ones.’ (64)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَّا فَتَحُوا۟ مَتَـٰعَهُمْ وَجَدُوا۟ بِضَـٰعَتَهُمْ رُدَّتْ إِلَيْهِمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مَا نَبْغِى ۖ هَـٰذِهِۦ بِضَـٰعَتُنَا رُدَّتْ إِلَيْنَا ۖ وَنَمِيرُ أَهْلَنَا وَنَحْفَظُ أَخَانَا وَنَزْدَادُ كَيْلَ بَعِيرٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ كَيْلٌ يَسِيرٌ (٦٥)
```

```english
And when they opened their baggage, they found their money restored to them. They said, ‘Father, what [more] do we want?! This is our money, restored to us! We will get provisions for our family and take care of our brother, and add another camel-load of rations. These are meagre rations.’ (65)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لَنْ أُرْسِلَهُۥ مَعَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ تُؤْتُونِ مَوْثِقًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ لَتَأْتُنَّنِى بِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يُحَاطَ بِكُمْ ۖ فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَوْهُ مَوْثِقَهُمْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَا نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ (٦٦)
```

```english
He said, ‘I will not let him go with you until you give me a [solemn] pledge by Allah that you will surely bring him back to me, unless you are made to perish.’ When they had given him their [solemn] pledge, he said, ‘Allah is witness over what we say.’ (66)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ يَـٰبَنِىَّ لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ مِنۢ بَابٍ وَٰحِدٍ وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ مِنْ أَبْوَٰبٍ مُّتَفَرِّقَةٍ ۖ وَمَآ أُغْنِى عَنكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۖ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۖ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ ۖ وَعَلَيْهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ (٦٧)
```

```english
And he said, ‘My sons, do not enter by one gate, but enter by separate gates, though I cannot avail you anything against Allah. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah. In Him I have put my trust; and in Him let all the trusting put their trust.’ (67)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَهُمْ أَبُوهُم مَّا كَانَ يُغْنِى عَنْهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا حَاجَةً فِى نَفْسِ يَعْقُوبَ قَضَىٰهَا ۚ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَذُو عِلْمٍ لِّمَا عَلَّمْنَـٰهُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦٨)
```

```english
When they entered whence their father had bidden them, it did not avail them anything against Allah, but only fulfilled a wish in Jacob’s heart. Indeed, he had the knowledge of what We had taught him, but most people do not know. (68)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ ءَاوَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَخَاهُ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ أَخُوكَ فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
When they entered into the presence of Joseph, he set his brother close to himself, and said, ‘Indeed I am your brother, so do not sorrow for what they used to do.’ (69)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم بِجَهَازِهِمْ جَعَلَ ٱلسِّقَايَةَ فِى رَحْلِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ أَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ أَيَّتُهَا ٱلْعِيرُ إِنَّكُمْ لَسَـٰرِقُونَ (٧٠)
```

```english
When he had furnished them with their provision, he put the drinking-cup into his brother’s saddlebag. Then a herald shouted: ‘O [men of the] caravan! You are indeed thieves!’ (70)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ وَأَقْبَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِم مَّاذَا تَفْقِدُونَ (٧١)
```

```english
They said, as they turned towards them, ‘What are you missing?’ (71)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ نَفْقِدُ صُوَاعَ ٱلْمَلِكِ وَلِمَن جَآءَ بِهِۦ حِمْلُ بَعِيرٍ وَأَنَا۠ بِهِۦ زَعِيمٌ (٧٢)
```

```english
They said, ‘We miss the king’s goblet.’ ‘Whoever brings it shall have a camel-load [of grain],’ [said the steward], ‘I will guarantee that.’ (72)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُم مَّا جِئْنَا لِنُفْسِدَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كُنَّا سَـٰرِقِينَ (٧٣)
```

```english
They said, ‘By Allah! You certainly know that we did not come to make trouble in this country, and we are not thieves.’ (73)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ فَمَا جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥٓ إِن كُنتُمْ كَـٰذِبِينَ (٧٤)
```

```english
They said, ‘What shall be its requital if you [prove to] be lying?’ (74)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥ مَن وُجِدَ فِى رَحْلِهِۦ فَهُوَ جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٧٥)
```

```english
They said, ‘The requital for it shall be that he in whose saddlebag it is found shall give himself over as its requital. Thus do we requite the wrongdoers.’ (75)
```

```arabic
فَبَدَأَ بِأَوْعِيَتِهِمْ قَبْلَ وِعَآءِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَخْرَجَهَا مِن وِعَآءِ أَخِيهِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كِدْنَا لِيُوسُفَ ۖ مَا كَانَ لِيَأْخُذَ أَخَاهُ فِى دِينِ ٱلْمَلِكِ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ مَّن نَّشَآءُ ۗ وَفَوْقَ كُلِّ ذِى عِلْمٍ عَلِيمٌ (٧٦)
```

```english
Then he began with their sacks, before [opening] his brother’s sack. Then he took it out from his brother’s sack. Thus did We devise for Joseph’s sake. He could not have held his brother under the king’s law unless Allah willed [otherwise]. We raise in rank whomever We please, and above every man of knowledge is One who knows best. (76)
```

```arabic
۞ قَالُوٓا۟ إِن يَسْرِقْ فَقَدْ سَرَقَ أَخٌ لَّهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ فَأَسَرَّهَا يُوسُفُ فِى نَفْسِهِۦ وَلَمْ يُبْدِهَا لَهُمْ ۚ قَالَ أَنتُمْ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا ۖ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَصِفُونَ (٧٧)
```

```english
They said, ‘If he has stolen [there is no wonder]; a brother of his had stolen before.’ Thereupon Joseph kept the matter to himself and he did not disclose it to them. He said, ‘You are in a worse state! And Allah knows best what you allege.’ (77)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ إِنَّ لَهُۥٓ أَبًا شَيْخًا كَبِيرًا فَخُذْ أَحَدَنَا مَكَانَهُۥٓ ۖ إِنَّا نَرَىٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٧٨)
```

```english
They said, ‘O emir! Indeed, he has a father, a very old man; so take one of us in his place. Indeed we see that you are a virtuous man.’ (78)
```

```arabic
قَالَ مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ أَن نَّأْخُذَ إِلَّا مَن وَجَدْنَا مَتَـٰعَنَا عِندَهُۥٓ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّظَـٰلِمُونَ (٧٩)
```

```english
He said, ‘God forbid that we should detain anyone except him with whom we found our wares, for then we would indeed be wrongdoers.’ (79)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا ٱسْتَيْـَٔسُوا۟ مِنْهُ خَلَصُوا۟ نَجِيًّا ۖ قَالَ كَبِيرُهُمْ أَلَمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ أَبَاكُمْ قَدْ أَخَذَ عَلَيْكُم مَّوْثِقًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمِن قَبْلُ مَا فَرَّطتُمْ فِى يُوسُفَ ۖ فَلَنْ أَبْرَحَ ٱلْأَرْضَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْذَنَ لِىٓ أَبِىٓ أَوْ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ لِى ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (٨٠)
```

```english
When they had despaired of [moving] him, they withdrew to confer privately. The eldest of them said, ‘Don’t you know that your father has taken a [solemn] pledge from you by Allah, and earlier you have neglected your duty in regard to Joseph? So I will never leave this land until my father permits me, or Allah passes a judgement for me, and He is the best of judges. (80)
```

```arabic
ٱرْجِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِيكُمْ فَقُولُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَآ إِنَّ ٱبْنَكَ سَرَقَ وَمَا شَهِدْنَآ إِلَّا بِمَا عَلِمْنَا وَمَا كُنَّا لِلْغَيْبِ حَـٰفِظِينَ (٨١)
```

```english
Go back to your father, and say, ‘‘Father! Your son has indeed committed theft, and we testified only to what we knew, and we could not have forestalled the unseen. (81)
```

```arabic
وَسْـَٔلِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ ٱلَّتِى كُنَّا فِيهَا وَٱلْعِيرَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَقْبَلْنَا فِيهَا ۖ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ (٨٢)
```

```english
Ask [the people of] the town we were in, and the caravan with which we came. We indeed speak the truth.’’ ’ (82)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses recount the return of Joseph’s (عليه‌السلام) brothers from him to their father, and their efforts to persuade their father to send Joseph’s maternal brother with them to obtain provisions (الاكتيال). It then describes their second journey to Joseph and how Joseph took his brother to himself through a stratagem he devised for that purpose.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَلَمَّا رَجَعُوا إِلى أَبِيهِمْ قالُوا يا أَبانا مُنِعَ مِنَّا الْكَيْلُ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنا أَخانا نَكْتَلْ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ — **"When they returned to their father, they said, ‘O father! The measure has been denied us. So send our brother with us, that we may obtain the measure, and we will indeed take care of him.’"** (12:63). Obtaining the measure (الاكتيال) refers to taking food by measure if it is of the type that is measured. Al-Raghib said: "The measure (الكيل) is the measuring of food. It is said: 'I measured (كلت) the food for him' if you took charge of that for him; and 'I measured (كلته) him the food' if you gave it to him by measure; and 'I obtained the measure (اكتلت) from him' if you took it from him by measure." The Exalted said: وَيْلٌ لِلْمُطَفِّفِينَ الَّذِينَ إِذَا اكْتالُوا عَلَى النَّاسِ يَسْتَوْفُونَ وَإِذا كالُوهُمْ — **"Woe to the defrauders! Who, when they take a measure from the people, take it in full, but when they measure for them..."** (83:1-3).

Their saying: قالُوا يا أَبانا مُنِعَ مِنَّا الْكَيْلُ — **"They said, ‘O father! The measure has been denied us’"** means: [it will be denied] if we do not take our brother and if he does not go with us to Egypt. This is evidenced by their saying: فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنا أَخانا — **"So send our brother with us."** This is a summary of what transpired between them and the Vizier of Egypt regarding his command to deny them the measure if they did not bring their paternal brother to him. They were recounting this to their father and asking him to send him with them so they could obtain the measure and not be deprived.

Their use of the term "our brother" (أَخانا) was a display of tenderness and compassion to put their father’s mind at ease regarding their intentions, as was their saying: وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ — **"and we will indeed take care of him,"** with its emphatic phrasing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالَ هَلْ آمَنُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا كَما أَمِنْتُكُمْ عَلى أَخِيهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَاللهُ خَيْرٌ حافِظاً وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ — **"He said, ‘Shall I trust you with him just as I trusted you with his brother before? Yet Allah is the best of guardians, and He is the most merciful of the merciful.’"** (12:64). It is mentioned in *al-Majma‘*: "Security (الأمن) is the heart’s tranquility (اطمئنان) regarding the safety of a matter." Thus, his saying: هَلْ آمَنُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ — **"Shall I trust you with him..."** etc., means: "Shall I feel tranquil toward you regarding this son of mine except in the same way I felt tranquil toward you regarding his brother Joseph before this, and then what happened, happened?"

The gist of it is: "You expect me to trust you with him and for my soul to feel tranquil toward you just as I trusted you and felt tranquil toward you regarding his brother before. You promise me by saying: 'and we will indeed take care of him' that you will protect him, just as you promised regarding Joseph by saying: 'and we will indeed take care of him.' I trusted you with a similar trust regarding Joseph, but you availed me nothing; you brought his shirt stained with blood, claiming the wolf had eaten him. My trusting you with this brother, like my trust with his brother before, is the trust of one whose trust and tranquility avail nothing, and in whose hand lies no protection for what is delivered to him or entrusted to him."

His saying: فَاللهُ خَيْرٌ حافِظاً وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ — **"Yet Allah is the best of guardians, and He is the most merciful of the merciful"** is a consequence of his previous statement: هَلْ آمَنُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ — **"Shall I trust you with him..."** etc. It conveys a conclusion: "Since tranquility toward you in his matter is futile, having no effect and availing nothing, then the best tranquility and reliance is that which is directed toward Allah, the Glorified, in terms of His protection. When the matter fluctuates between trust in Him (التوكل) and delegation to Him (التفويض) versus tranquility toward others, then trusting in Him, the Exalted, is the chosen and determined path."

His saying: وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ — **"and He is the most merciful of the merciful"** serves as the rationale for his saying: فَاللهُ خَيْرٌ حافِظاً — **"Yet Allah is the best of guardians."** That is, others besides Him, the Exalted, might be trusted with a matter or given a deposit (أمانة) to keep, yet they do not show mercy to the one who entrusted them and they lose the deposit. However, He, the Glorified, is the Most Merciful of the merciful; He does not withhold mercy where mercy is due, and He shows mercy to the helpless and weak who delegates his affair to Him and relies upon Him. And whoever relies upon Allah, He is sufficient for him.

From this, it becomes clear that his (عليه‌السلام) intent was not merely to state the necessity of choosing Him, the Exalted, for reliance because He is an independent cause (سبب مستقل) in His causality—never overcome—unlike all other causes, even if that is true. The Exalted said: وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ — **"And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him. Indeed Allah carries through His command."** (65:3). For how could it be otherwise, when tranquility toward other than Him, the Exalted, in this sense is a form of polytheism (الشرك) from which the station of the Prophets is sanctified? Allah has explicitly stated that Jacob (عليه‌السلام) was among the sincere (المخلصين), the chosen ones (أهل الاجتباء), and that he was among the guiding and guided leaders (الأئمة الهداة المهديين). Furthermore, he (عليه‌السلام) admits in his saying: إِلَّا كَما أَمِنْتُكُمْ عَلى أَخِيهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ — **"just as I trusted you with his brother before"** that he did trust them with Joseph; had this been polytheism, he would never have done it. Moreover, he trusted them with Joseph’s brother as well after they gave him a pledge before Allah, as the subsequent verses indicate.

Rather, he intended to state the necessity of choosing Him, the Exalted, for tranquility rather than others because He, the Exalted, is characterized by noble attributes that ensure He will not deceive His servants who rely upon Him and surrender their affairs to Him. For He is Kind to His servants, Merciful, Forgiving, Loving, Generous, Wise, and All-Knowing—all of which are encompassed by His being the Most Merciful of the merciful. Furthermore, He is never overcome in His command nor coerced in His will. As for people, when they are trusted with a matter, they are captives of whims and the playthings of psychological caprices. Sometimes noble character, the trait of loyalty, and the attribute of mercy take hold of them, and they protect what is in their choice to protect and do not betray it; at other times, they betray and do not protect. Moreover, they possess no independence in power nor self-sufficiency in strength or will.

In short, his (عليه‌السلام) meaning is that tranquility in the protection of Allah, the Glorified, is better than tranquility in the protection of others, because He, the Exalted, is the Most Merciful of the merciful; He does not betray His servant in what he has trusted Him with. This is unlike people, who may not fulfill the covenant of trust and may not show mercy to the one who entrusted them and sought their help, thus betraying him. Therefore, when he later required his sons to give him a pledge before Allah, he said: تُؤْتُونِ مَوْثِقاً مِنَ اللهِ لَتَأْتُنَّنِي بِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ يُحاطَ بِكُمْ — **"unless you are beset"** (12:66). He excepted that which was outside their choice regarding protection—namely, His protection if they were "beset" (أحيط بهم)—for that is beyond their ability and power, and they are not held responsible for it. He only asked them for a pledge to bring him back in matters that do not leave the realm of their choice, such as killing, exile, and the like. Understand this.

From what has preceded, it appears that in his (عليه‌السلام) saying: وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ — **"and He is the most merciful of the merciful,"** there is a kind of subtle reproach toward them and an allusion that they did not fulfill the requirements of mercy—or did not show him mercy at all—in the matter of Joseph when he trusted them with him. In any case, the verse serves as a rejection of what they requested.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَلَمَّا فَتَحُوا مَتاعَهُمْ وَجَدُوا بِضاعَتَهُمْ رُدَّتْ إِلَيْهِمْ — **"When they opened their baggage, they found their merchandise returned to them..."** (12:65) until the end of the verse. Seeking (البغي) is to search for something, and it is often used in an evil sense, from which comes transgression (البغي) meaning injustice, and (البغي) meaning fornication. It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "Provisions (الميرة) are the foods carried from one land to another. It is said: 'He provided them (مارهم) with provisions (ميرا)' if you brought them provisions; similarly: 'I obtained provisions from them (امترتهم امتيارا).'"

Their saying: يا أَبانا ما نَبْغِي — **"They said, ‘O father! What [more] do we desire?’"** is an interrogative. That is, when they opened their baggage and found their merchandise returned to them, it was evidence of the Vizier’s generosity toward them and that he intended no harm. Since the food had been delivered to them and the price returned, their journey to Egypt for provisions (الامتيار) was the best of journeys in terms of benefit and abundance. They returned to their father and said: "O father! What else could we seek from our journey to Egypt beyond this? He has given us the full measure and returned to us the merchandise we offered as a price."

So their saying: يا أَبانا ما نَبْغِي هذِهِ بِضاعَتُنا رُدَّتْ إِلَيْنا — **"O father! What [more] do we desire? Here is our merchandise returned to us"** was intended to put their father’s mind at ease so he would agree to their brother going with them, as he would be safe from the Vizier and they would protect him as they promised. Therefore, they followed it with their saying: وَنَمِيرُ أَهْلَنا وَنَحْفَظُ أَخانا وَنَزْدادُ كَيْلَ بَعِيرٍ ذلِكَ كَيْلٌ يَسِيرٌ — **"We will get provisions for our family and take care of our brother, and we will add a camel-load [to our stock]. That is an easy measure."** (12:65), meaning it is simple to obtain.

It has also been said that the word "what" (ما) in his saying: ما نَبْغِي — **"What [more] do we desire"** is for negation, meaning: "We do not seek (ما نطلب) any falsehood in what we told you about the Vizier and his generosity toward us; here is our merchandise returned to us." Similarly, it has been said that "easy" (يسير) means "small," meaning: "The measure we have brought you is small and does not suffice us, so we need to add to it the camel-load of our brother."

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالَ لَنْ أُرْسِلَهُ مَعَكُمْ حَتَّى تُؤْتُونِ مَوْثِقاً مِنَ اللهِ لَتَأْتُنَّنِي بِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ يُحاطَ بِكُمْ فَلَمَّا آتَوْهُ مَوْثِقَهُمْ قالَ اللهُ عَلى ما نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ — **"He said, ‘I will not send him with you until you give me a pledge before Allah that you will surely bring him back to me, unless you are beset.’ When they had given him their pledge, he said, ‘Allah is witness over what we say.’"** (12:66). A pledge (الموثق) is that which is relied upon and trusted. A pledge before Allah (الموثق من الله) is a matter that is trusted and simultaneously linked to Allah. Giving a divine pledge means granting someone power over a divine matter that is trusted, such as a covenant (العهد) or an oath (اليمين), which acts like a security (رهينة). The one who makes a covenant or takes an oath by saying, "I covenant with Allah to do such-and-half" or "By Allah, I shall surely do such-and-such," is pledging the honor and sanctity of Allah as a security with the one he covenants with or swears to. If he does not fulfill what he said, he loses his security and is inevitably accountable before Allah.

Besetting (الإحاطة) comes from (حاط) meaning to protect, from which comes (الحائط) for a wall that surrounds a place to protect it. Allah, the Glorified, encompasses (محيط) everything, meaning He has mastery over it and preserves it from every side; nothing, nor any part of it, escapes His power. "Affliction and calamity beset him" (أحاط به) means it descended upon him such that all paths of escape were blocked, leaving him no way out. From this comes the expression "he was beset" (أحيط به), meaning he perished, was ruined, or the paths of salvation were closed to him. The Exalted said: وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِ فَأَصْبَحُ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ عَلى ما أَنْفَقَ فِيها — **"And his produce was all swept away. Thereupon he began to wring his hands for what he had spent on it..."** (18:42). And He said: وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ دَعَوُا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ — **"and they think they are beset, they call upon Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him..."** (10:22). From this is His saying in the verse: إِلَّا أَنْ يُحاطَ بِكُمْ — **"unless you are beset,"** meaning unless a calamity befalls you that strips you of all ability and power, such that you are unable to bring him back to me.

Trusteeship (الوكالة) is a type of mastery over a matter belonging to another in order to carry it out. A person’s appointing another as a trustee (توكيل) in a matter is giving him mastery over it to stand in his place in setting it right. Relying upon Him (التوكل عليه) is depending on Him and feeling tranquil toward Him in a matter. Appointing Him, the Exalted, as a trustee and relying upon Him in affairs is not merely because He is the Creator of everything, its Owner, and its Disposer, but rather because He has permitted the attribution of matters to their sources and actions to their agents, granting them ownership of these actions in a certain way, while they lack originality and independence in influence. Allah, the Glorified, is the independent Cause who dominates every cause and overcomes it. Thus, it is a sign of right-guidance (الرشد) that when a person desires a matter and pursues it through the ordinary causes at his disposal, he sees Allah, the Glorified, as the sole independent Cause in disposing of the matter, and he negates independence and originality from himself and from the causes he employed. This is relying upon Him (التوكل عليه). Thus, reliance (التوكل) is not a person’s severing or negating the attribution of matters to himself or to the causes; rather, it is his negation of the claim of independence for himself and for the causes, and returning independence and originality to Him, the Exalted, while maintaining the basic non-independent attribution to himself and to the causes.

Therefore, we see that Jacob (عليه‌السلام), in what the verses recount of his reliance upon Allah, did not nullify the causes nor neglect them. Rather, he held fast to ordinary causes: he first spoke to his sons regarding their brother, then took from them a pledge before Allah, then relied upon Allah. Similarly, in what he advised them in the following verse regarding entering from separate gates, he then relied upon his Lord, the Exalted.

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is a Trustee (وكيل) over everything from the perspective of the matters attributed to them, just as He is their Guardian (ولي) from the perspective of His independence in maintaining the matters attributed to them while they are unable to maintain them by any power or strength, and He is the Lord (رب) of everything from the perspective that He is their Owner and Disposer.

The meaning of the verse: قالَ — **"He said,"** i.e., Jacob to his sons: لَنْ أُرْسِلَهُ — **"I will not send him,"** i.e., your brother from Joseph’s mother, مَعَكُمْ حَتَّى تُؤْتُونِ — **"with you until you give me"** مَوْثِقاً مِنَ اللهِ — **"a pledge before Allah"** that I can trust and rely upon, such as a covenant or an oath, لَتَأْتُنَّنِي بِهِ — **"that you will surely bring him back to me."** The (لام) is for an oath. Since their giving a pledge before Allah only holds and avails in what pertains to their ability and power, he made an exception, saying: إِلَّا أَنْ يُحاطَ بِكُمْ — **"unless you are beset"** and stripped of ability and power. فَلَمَّا آتَوْهُ مَوْثِقَهُمْ — **"When they had given him their pledge"** before Allah, قالَ — **"he said,"** Jacob: اللهُ عَلى ما نَقُولُ وَكيلٌ — **"Allah is witness over what we say."** That is: "We have all engaged in this discourse—I have spoken and you have spoken—and we have used this as a means through these ordinary causes to reach a goal we seek. So let Allah, the Glorified, be the Trustee over these words, allowing them to proceed according to their course. Whoever has committed to something must fulfill it as he committed, and if he fails, let Allah judge him and take justice from him."

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَقالَ يا بَنِيَّ لا تَدْخُلُوا مِنْ بابٍ واحِدٍ وَادْخُلُوا مِنْ أَبْوابٍ مُتَفَرِّقَةٍ — **"And he said, ‘O my sons, do not enter by one gate, but enter by separate gates...’"** (12:67) until the end of the verse. This was a word Jacob (عليه‌السلام) addressed to his sons when they gave him the pledge before Allah and had prepared and readied themselves for the journey. It is clear from the context of the story that he feared for his sons—who were a group (عصبة) of eleven—not that the Vizier of Egypt would see them gathered in one line, for it was known that he would summon them to him and they would stand before him in one line as eleven brothers from one father. Rather, he feared that the people would see them and they would be afflicted by the evil eye (العين), as has been said, or be envied, or be feared, resulting in something that would disperse their gathering, such as killing or any other calamity.

His saying afterward: وَما أُغْنِي عَنْكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ — **"I cannot avail you anything against Allah. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah"** is not devoid of indication or suggestion that he feared this greatly. It is as if he (عليه‌السلام)—and Allah knows best—sensed through an inspired feeling when they prepared for the journey and stood before him to bid farewell, that their gathering in this handsome form would be dispersed and their number diminished. So he commanded them not to display their gathering in that way, warned them against entering from one gate, and insisted they enter from separate gates, hoping that this would repel the affliction of separation and the reduction of their number.

Then he returned to the literal implication of his speech, which might suggest that this cause he relied upon to repel the calamity he sensed was an original and independent cause—whereas in reality, there is no influencer in existence except Allah, the Glorified. So he qualified his speech with what corrects it, saying to them: وَما أُغْنِي عَنْكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — **"I cannot avail you anything against Allah."** He then reasoned this by saying: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ — **"Sovereignty belongs only to Allah."** That is: "I do not remove your need for Allah, the Glorified, through what I have commanded you of the cause by which you guard against the descent of calamity and seek safety and well-being. Nor do I decree that you will be protected by this stratagem, for these causes avail nothing against Allah, nor do they have any sovereignty (حكم) apart from Allah. Sovereignty is not absolute except for Allah; rather, these are outward causes that only influence if Allah wills them to influence."

Therefore, he followed this speech with his saying: عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَعَلَيْهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ — **"In Him I have put my trust, and in Him let all the trusting put their trust."** That is: "This is a cause I have commanded you to take to repel the affliction I fear for you, and I have simultaneously relied upon Allah in taking this cause and in all other causes I have taken in my affairs. Upon this path must every rightly-guided person walk—one who is not astray—who sees that he cannot, by his own independence, manage his affairs, nor can ordinary causes, by their independence, bring him to the goals he seeks. Rather, he must take refuge in his affairs with a Trustee (وكيل) who sets his state right and disposes of his affair in the best manner. That Trustee is Allah, the Glorified, the Subduer whom nothing subdues, the Victor whom nothing overcomes; He does what He wills and decrees what He intends."

From the verse, it has become clear, firstly: the meaning of reliance (التوكل), which is giving another mastery over a matter that has an attribution to the one relying (المتوكل) and the one appointing the trustee (الموكل).

Secondly: Since these ordinary causes are not independent in their influence nor self-sufficient in their essence—not being independent of what lies beyond them—it is mandatory for whoever employs them for his vital goals to rely, while employing them, upon a Cause beyond them so that their influence may be completed. This would be a course of right-guidance and correctness, rather than neglecting the causes upon which Allah built the system of the universe and seeking a goal through other than its path, for that is a form of error and ignorance.

Thirdly: That Cause upon which one must rely in affairs is Allah, the Glorified, alone without partner, for He is Allah, there is no god but He, the Lord of everything. This is what is understood from the restriction (الحصر) indicated by His saying: "and in Him let all the trusting put their trust."

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَهُمْ أَبُوهُمْ ما كانَ يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا حاجَةً فِي نَفْسِ يَعْقُوبَ قَضاها — **"And when they entered as their father had bidden them, it did not avail them anything against Allah, but [it was only] a need in Jacob’s soul, which he satisfied..."** (12:68) until the end of the verse. What the context of the preceding and following verses and reflection upon them suggests—and Allah knows best—is that their entering from where their father commanded them means they entered Egypt or the house of the Vizier therein from separate gates, as their father commanded them when they bid him farewell. Jacob (عليه‌السلام) only took this command as a means to repel what he had intuited of a calamity descending upon them that would disperse their gathering and reduce their number, as alluded to in the previous verse. However, taking this means—namely, entering as their father commanded them—was not to repel the affliction from them, for Allah’s decree was carried out upon them. The Vizier took their brother from their father due to the incident of the theft of the goblet, and their eldest brother separated from them and remained in Egypt. This led to the dispersal of their gathering and the reduction of their number. Thus, neither Jacob nor the act of entering as he commanded availed them anything against Allah.

However, Allah, the Glorified, thereby satisfied a need in Jacob’s (عليه‌السلام) soul. For He made this very cause—which failed in its intended purpose and led to the dispersal of their gathering and the reduction of their number—the cause for Jacob reaching Joseph (عليه‌السلام). For Joseph took his brother to himself, and the other brothers returned, except for their eldest, to their father. Then they returned to Joseph, seeking mercy and humbling themselves before his might, so he made himself known to them and summoned his father and family to Egypt, and they were reunited with him.

So His saying: ما كانَ يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — **"it did not avail them anything against Allah"** means it was not within the capacity of Jacob or this matter he took as a means for their escape from this descending calamity to avail them anything against Allah at all, nor to repel what Allah had decreed—that two of them would be separated from their gathering. Rather, one was taken from them and another, their eldest, remained in the land of Egypt.

His saying: إِلَّا حاجَةً فِي نَفْسِ يَعْقُوبَ قَضاها — **"but [it was only] a need in Jacob’s soul, which he satisfied"**: it has been said that "but" (إِلَّا) here means "however" (لكن), i.e., "however, there was a need in Jacob’s soul which Allah satisfied," so He returned to him his lost son, Joseph.

It is not unlikely that "but" (إِلَّا) is for exception, for His saying: "it did not avail them anything against Allah" is in the sense of our saying: "This cause did not benefit Jacob at all," or "it did not benefit them all at all, and Allah did not satisfy any need for them all through it, except for a need in Jacob’s soul." His saying: قَضاها — **"which he satisfied"** is a new sentence (استئناف) and an answer to a hypothetical question: "What did He do with it?" The answer was: "He satisfied it."

His saying: وَإِنَّهُ لَذُو عِلْمٍ لِما عَلَّمْناهُ — **"and indeed he possessed a knowledge for what We had taught him"**: the pronoun refers to Jacob. That is, Jacob possessed knowledge because of the knowledge We taught him, or because of Our teaching him. The explicit attribution of teaching to Him, the Exalted, indicates that it was a bestowed knowledge (علم موهبي), not an acquired one (اكتسابي). It has been mentioned previously that sincerity in monotheism (إخلاص التوحيد) leads to such divine care. This is also supported by His saying afterward: وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — **"but most people do not know,"** for if it were acquired knowledge that judges by outward causes and is reached through familiar ordinary paths, people would have known it and been guided to it.

The sentence: وَإِنَّهُ لَذُو عِلْمٍ لِما عَلَّمْناهُ — **"and indeed he possessed a knowledge for what We had taught him"** etc., is a praise of Jacob (عليه‌السلام). Bestowed knowledge does not stray in its guidance nor err in its aim. The discourse, as the context suggests, refers to what Jacob (عليه‌السلام) intuited of the affliction, the means he employed, and his need regarding Joseph in his soul, which he never forgot and continued to remember. From these perspectives, it is known that in His saying: "and indeed he possessed a knowledge for what We had taught him" etc., there is a confirmation of Jacob (عليه‌السلام) in what he said to his sons and a validation of the means he took for his need by commanding them as he did and his reliance upon Allah, so Allah satisfied for him a need in his soul.

This is what reflection upon the context of the verses yields. The commentators have strange opinions regarding the meaning of the verse, such as the saying of some: that the meaning of His saying: "it did not avail them... which he satisfied" is that their entering as their father commanded them did not avail them or repel from them anything Allah intended to befall them of envy or the evil eye. Jacob (عليه‌السلام) was aware that caution does not repel destiny, but what he said to his sons was a need in his soul, so Jacob satisfied that need, i.e., he removed the agitation of his heart and dispelled the anxiety from his soul.

And the saying of some: that the meaning is that if Allah had decreed that the evil eye should strike them, it would have struck them while they were separate just as it would have struck them while they were together.

And the saying of some: that the meaning of His saying: "and indeed he possessed a knowledge for what We had taught him" etc., is that he possessed certainty and gnosis of Allah because of Our teaching him, but most people do not know his rank.

And the saying of some: that the (لام) in لِما عَلَّمْناهُ — **"for what We had taught him"** is for emphasis (للتقوية), and the meaning is that he knows what We taught him and acts upon it, because whoever knows something but does not act upon it is like one who does not know. There are other such opinions of theirs.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا عَلى يُوسُفَ آوى إِلَيْهِ أَخاهُ قالَ إِنِّي أَنَا أَخُوكَ فَلا تَبْتَئِسْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — **"When they entered into Joseph’s presence, he took his brother into his arms and said, ‘I am indeed your brother. So do not be saddened by what they used to do.’"** (12:69). Taking him into his arms (الإيواء إليه) means drawing him close and bringing him near to him in his assembly and the like. Being saddened (الابتئاس) is to bring upon oneself misery, grief, and sorrow. The plural pronoun refers to the brothers.

The meaning of the verse: وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا عَلى يُوسُفَ — **"When they entered into Joseph’s presence"** after entering Egypt, آوى — **"he took into his arms"** and brought near إِلَيْهِ أَخاهُ — **"his brother"** whom he had commanded them to bring to him, and he was his brother from his father and mother. قالَ — **"he said"** to him: إِنِّي أَنَا أَخُوكَ — **"I am indeed your brother,"** i.e., Joseph whom you lost years ago—the sentence is a predicate after a predicate or an answer to a hypothetical question. فَلا تَبْتَئِسْ — **"So do not be saddened"** and do not grieve بِما كانُوا — **"by what they,"** i.e., the brothers, يَعْمَلُونَ — **"used to do"** of various types of harm and injustices that their envy drove them to commit against me and you, as we are brothers from one mother. Or: "do not be saddened by what my servants will do, for it is a plot to keep you with me."

The apparent context is that he made himself known to him by speaking to him in secret, and he consoled him for what the brothers had done and put his mind at ease. Thus, no weight should be given to the saying of some that the meaning of his saying: "I am indeed your brother" is: "I am your brother in place of your perished brother"—for he had told him that he had a brother from his mother who perished before, leaving him alone without a maternal brother—and that Joseph did not admit the lineage to him but only wanted to put his mind at ease.

This is because it is contradicted by the various forms of emphasis in his saying: "I am indeed your brother," which is only suitable for making himself known through lineage so that he might be certain that he is indeed Joseph. Furthermore, it also contradicts what will come of his saying to his brothers when he makes himself known to them: "I am Joseph, and this is my brother; Allah has certainly been gracious to us." This is only suitable if his brother already knew that he was his brother and thus felt honored by his honor, as is not hidden.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُمْ بِجَهازِهِمْ جَعَلَ السِّقايَةَ فِي رَحْلِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ أَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ أَيَّتُهَا الْعِيرُ إِنَّكُمْ لَسارِقُونَ — **"When he had furnished them with their provisions, he put the drinking-cup into his brother’s saddlebag. Then a herald shouted, ‘O [men of the] caravan! You are indeed thieves!’"** (12:70). The drinking-cup (السقاية) is the vessel from which one drinks. The saddlebag (الرحل) is what is placed on the camel for riding. The caravan (العير) refers to the people who have loads of provisions; it is a name for the men and the camels carrying the provisions, though it may be used for either one without the other, as mentioned by al-Raghib in his *Mufradat*.

The meaning of the verse is clear. This was a stratagem Joseph (عليه‌السلام) devised to take his brother to himself, as Allah recounted and detailed, making it a prelude to making himself known to them while his brother had joined him, both of them enjoying Allah’s blessing and honored by His honor.

His saying: ثُمَّ أَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ أَيَّتُهَا الْعِيرُ إِنَّكُمْ لَسارِقُونَ — **"Then a herald shouted, ‘O [men of the] caravan! You are indeed thieves!’"**: the address is to Joseph’s brothers, among whom was his maternal brother. It is permissible to direct an address to a group regarding a matter that pertains to some of them if they are not distinguished from the others; there is much of this in the Qur’an. This matter which was called theft—namely, the presence of the drinking-cup in the camel’s saddlebag—was present with one of them, Joseph’s maternal brother. However, his not being identified yet from among them made it permissible to address them all by saying: "You are indeed thieves." For the meaning of this address in such a context is that the drinking-cup is missing and it is with one of you who cannot be identified except after search and inspection.

It is known from the context that Joseph’s maternal brother was aware of this plot and mindful of it. Therefore, he did not speak from the beginning to the end, not even a word, nor did he deny the theft from himself, nor was he agitated. How could he be, when Joseph had made himself known to him as his brother, consoled him, and put his mind at ease? It must be that Joseph (عليه‌السلام) had informed him of his purpose in this act, and that by calling him a thief and extracting the drinking-cup from his saddlebag, he only intended to seize him and take him to himself. Thus, calling him a thief was only an accusation in the eyes of the brothers; as for him and in his view, it was not a serious naming or a true accusation, but merely a formal description for a necessary and determined benefit.

Thus, attributing theft to them—considering these perspectives—was not of the type of slander (الافتراء) that is intellectually blameworthy and legally prohibited. Furthermore, the speaker was the herald who announced it.

Some commentators mentioned that the one who said: "You are indeed thieves" was one of those who missed the goblet from Joseph’s people, without him commanding it, and he did not know that Joseph had ordered the goblet to be placed in their saddlebags.

Others said that Joseph (عليه‌السلام) commanded the herald to announce it but did not intend the theft of the goblet; rather, he meant: "You stole Joseph from his father and threw him into the pit." This was attributed to Abu Muslim the commentator.

Others said that the sentence is interrogative, and the implied meaning is: "Are you indeed thieves?" by omitting the interrogative hamza. The far-fetched nature of these views is not hidden.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالُوا وَأَقْبَلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ ما ذا تَفْقِدُونَ — **"Turning to them, they said, ‘What is it that you have lost?’"** (12:71). Loss (الفقد)—as has been said—is the absence of a thing from the senses such that its location is unknown. The pronoun in his saying: قالُوا — **"they said"** refers to the brothers, who are the caravan. His saying: ما ذا تَفْقِدُونَ — **"What is it that you have lost?"** is the quoted speech. The pronoun in his saying: عَلَيْهِمْ — **"to them"** refers to Joseph and his servants, as indicated by the context.

The meaning is: Joseph’s brothers, turning toward Joseph and his servants, said: "What is it that you have lost?" The context indicates that the herald only called them from behind them after they had started their journey.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالُوا نَفْقِدُ صُواعَ الْمَلِكِ وَلِمَنْ جاءَ بِهِ حِمْلُ بَعِيرٍ وَأَنَا بِهِ زَعِيمٌ — **"They said, ‘We have lost the king’s goblet. Whoever brings it shall have a camel-load [of grain], and I will be its guarantor.’"** (12:72). The goblet (الصواع) with a *damma* is the drinking-cup (السقاية). It has been said that the *suwa‘* is the *sa‘* (measure) with which one measures. The king’s goblet was a vessel from which one drank and with which one measured; therefore, it was called at times a drinking-cup (سقاية) and at others a goblet (صواع). It can be treated as masculine or feminine; therefore, He said: وَلِمَنْ جاءَ بِهِ — **"Whoever brings it (masc.)"** and He said: ثُمَّ اسْتَخْرَجَها — **"Then he extracted it (fem.)."**

The load (الحمل) is the weight carried by a carrier. Al-Raghib mentioned that weights carried outwardly, like something carried on the back, are specifically called *himl* with a *kasra* on the *ha*, while weights carried inwardly, like a child in the womb, water in the clouds, or fruit on a tree, are specifically called *haml* with a *fatha* on the *ha*.

It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "The guarantor (الزعيم), the surety (الكفيل), and the bondsman (الضمين) are synonyms. The *za‘im* is also the one who stands in charge of the people’s affairs, i.e., the leader."

Perhaps the one who said: نَفْقِدُ صُواعَ الْمَلِكِ — **"We have lost the king’s goblet"** were Joseph’s servants, and the one who said: وَلِمَنْ جاءَ بِهِ حِمْلُ بَعِيرٍ وَأَنَا بِهِ زَعِيمٌ — **"Whoever brings it shall have a camel-load, and I will be its guarantor"** was Joseph (عليه‌السلام) himself, because he was the leader who was in charge of giving, withholding, guaranteeing, suretyship, and judgment. The meaning of the speech, according to this, would be: Joseph and his servants answered them; as for his servants, they said: "We have lost the king’s goblet," and as for Joseph, he said: "Whoever brings it shall have a camel-load, and I will be its guarantor." This is a reward (جعالة).

It appears from some commentators that his saying: "Whoever brings it shall have a camel-load, and I will be its guarantor" is a continuation of the herald’s speech: "O [men of the] caravan! You are indeed thieves!" According to this, His saying: "Turning to them, they said... the king’s goblet" is a parenthetical statement.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالُوا تَاللهِ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمْ ما جِئْنا لِنُفْسِدَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَما كُنَّا سارِقِينَ — **"They said, ‘By Allah! You certainly know that we did not come to cause corruption in the land, and we have not been thieves.’"** (12:73). The land (الأرض) refers to the land of Egypt, which they had come to. The meaning of the verse is clear.

In their saying: لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمْ ما جِئْنا لِنُفْسِدَ فِي الْأَرْضِ — **"You certainly know that we did not come to cause corruption in the land,"** there is an indication that they were searched and investigated regarding their affair when they first entered Egypt for provisions by Joseph’s (عليه‌السلام) command, under the claim of fear that they might be spies and scouts or had come for other corrupt purposes. They were asked about their state, their place, their lineage, and the like. This supports what has been mentioned in some narrations that Joseph showed them that he was suspicious of their affair, so he asked them about their state, their place, and their family, at which point they mentioned that they had an elderly father and a paternal brother, so he commanded them to bring him. This will come in the following traditional discussion, if Allah wills.

Their saying: وَما كُنَّا سارِقِينَ — **"and we have not been thieves"** is a negation of their being characterized by this vile trait before, or that such a thing was known of them, the People of the House.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالُوا فَما جَزاؤُهُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ كاذِبِينَ — **"They said, ‘Then what shall be its penalty, if you are liars?’"** (12:74). That is, Joseph’s servants, or he and his servants, said, asking them about the penalty: "What is the penalty for the theft, or what is the penalty for the one who stole from you, if you are liars in your denial?"

The discourse regarding their saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ كاذِبِينَ — **"if you are liars"** in attributing lying to them is similar to the discourse regarding their saying: "You are indeed thieves," which has preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالُوا جَزاؤُهُ مَنْ وُجِدَ فِي رَحْلِهِ فَهُوَ جَزاؤُهُ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الظَّالِمِينَ — **"They said, ‘Its penalty is that he in whose saddlebag it is found shall himself be its penalty. Thus do we punish the wrongdoers.’"** (12:75). Their meaning was that the penalty for the theft is the thief himself, or the penalty for the thief is himself, meaning that whoever steals property becomes a slave to the one whose property he stole. Such was the judgment in the tradition (سنة) of Jacob (عليه‌السلام), as indicated by their saying: كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الظَّالِمِينَ — **"Thus do we punish the wrongdoers,"** meaning these wrongdoers who are the thieves. However, they phrased it as: "Its penalty is that he in whose saddlebag it is found shall himself be its penalty" to indicate that the theft is only penalized through the thief himself, not his companions and associates—who were eleven souls—who should not be held responsible if the theft were proven except for the thief specifically, without it extending to the others and their saddlebags. Then the one stolen from has the right to own the thief himself and do with him as he pleases.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَبَدأَ بِأَوْعِيَتِهِمْ قَبْلَ وِعاءِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ اسْتَخْرَجَها مِنْ وِعاءِ أَخِيهِ — **"Then he began with their baggage before his brother’s baggage, then he extracted it from his brother’s baggage..."** (12:76). This is a consequence of what preceded, meaning he began the inspection and search based on the penalty they had mentioned. He began with their baggage and vessels before his brother’s vessel to mislead them, fearing they might notice and realize that he was the one who placed it in his brother’s saddlebag. Then he extracted it from his brother’s vessel, and at that point, the penalty was established upon him because it was in his saddlebag.

**His saying, the Exalted:** كَذلِكَ كِدْنا لِيُوسُفَ ما كانَ لِيَأْخُذَ أَخاهُ فِي دِينِ الْمَلِكِ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ — **"Thus did We stratagem for Joseph. He could not have taken his brother under the king’s law, unless Allah wished..."** (12:76) until the end of the verse. The reference is to what transpired in the course of Joseph’s (عليه‌السلام) taking his maternal brother from the group of his brothers. It was a stratagem (كيد) because it led to what he sought from them without them knowing or realizing it; had they known, they would not have agreed to it nor enabled him to do it. This is the stratagem, except that it was by inspiration from Allah, the Glorified, or revelation from Him to him, teaching him the way to reach the taking of his brother. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, attributed that to Himself while describing it as a stratagem, saying: "Thus did We stratagem for Joseph."

Not every stratagem is negated from Him, the Exalted; rather, His holy presence is sanctified from the stratagem that is injustice. Its counterparts are plotting (المكر), misleading (الإضلال), gradual enticement (الاستدراج), and others.

His saying: ما كانَ لِيَأْخُذَ أَخاهُ فِي دِينِ الْمَلِكِ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ — **"He could not have taken his brother under the king’s law, unless Allah wished"** is an explanation of the reason prompting the stratagem. It was that he wanted to take his brother to himself, and there was no way in the king’s law—i.e., his current tradition in the land of Egypt—that led to taking him. Nor was the penalty for theft the enslavement of the thief. Therefore, Joseph—by command from Allah—plotted against them by placing the drinking-cup in his saddlebag, then announcing that they were thieves until they denied it, then asking them about its penalty if they were liars, so they informed him that the penalty for theft among them was the taking and enslavement of the thief. Thus, he took them by what they had accepted for themselves.

Accordingly, he could not have taken his brother under the king’s law except in a state where Allah willed that, which is this state in which they agreed to be penalized by what they accepted for themselves.

From this, it appears that the exception indicates that it was part of the king’s law that a criminal be taken by the penalty he accepts for himself, which is more severe. This was common in many national traditions and royal policies.

His saying: نَرْفَعُ دَرَجاتٍ مَنْ نَشاءُ وَفَوْقَ كُلِّ ذِي عِلْمٍ عَلِيمٌ — **"We raise in rank whomever We wish, and above every possessor of knowledge is one who is all-knowing."** (12:76) is a grace upon Joseph (عليه‌السلام) for how Allah raised him above his brothers, and an explanation for His saying: "Thus did We stratagem for Joseph," which was a grace upon him.

In His saying: وَفَوْقَ كُلِّ ذِي عِلْمٍ عَلِيمٌ — **"and above every possessor of knowledge is one who is all-knowing,"** there is a statement that knowledge is among the matters that do not stop at a limit where it ends; rather, for every possessor of knowledge, one can suppose someone who is more knowledgeable than him.

It should be known that the literal meaning of "possessor of knowledge" (ذِي عِلْمٍ) is knowledge that is accidental to the knower and additional to his essence, because of the word "possessor" (ذِي) which indicates accompaniment and association. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, and His knowledge—which is an attribute of His essence—is identical to His essence. He, the Exalted, is a knowledge that is unlimited, just as His existence is unitary and unlimited, being by His essence outside the absolute scope of the discourse.

Furthermore, the sentence "and above every possessor of knowledge is one who is all-knowing" only holds true where it is possible to suppose an "above" (فَوْقَ). Allah, the Glorified, has no above, no below, no "behind" to His existence, no limit to His essence, and no end.

It is not unlikely that His saying: "and above every possessor of knowledge is one who is all-knowing" is an allusion to His being, the Exalted, above every possessor of knowledge, such that the intended meaning of "all-knowing" (عليم) is Allah, the Glorified, mentioned in the indefinite form to protect the tongue from defining Him, out of veneration.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالُوا إِنْ يَسْرِقْ فَقَدْ سَرَقَ أَخٌ لَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ — **"They said, ‘If he has stolen, a brother of his has stolen before.’"** (12:77) until the end of the verse. The speakers are Joseph’s (عليه‌السلام) paternal brothers. Therefore, they attributed Joseph to their brother accused of theft because they were from one mother. The meaning is that they said: "If this one steals the king’s goblet, it is not far-fetched for him, because he had a brother and theft was proven of him before; so they both inherit that from their mother’s side, while we are separate from them in terms of the mother."

In this is a type of exoneration for themselves from theft, but it is not devoid of contradicting what they said just now: "and we have not been thieves," because they were negating theft from all the sons of Jacob; otherwise, it would not have benefited them at all. So their saying: "a brother of his has stolen before" contradicts it, and this is clear. Furthermore, they displayed by this word the envy in their souls toward Joseph and his brother—and perhaps they did not realize it—and this reveals many regrettable matters among them.

By this, the meaning of Joseph’s saying: أَنْتُمْ شَرٌّ مَكاناً — **"You are in a worse plight"** becomes somewhat clear. It is also apparent that his saying: "You are in a worse plight" until the end of the verse is like an explanation for His saying: فَأَسَرَّها يُوسُفُ فِي نَفْسِهِ وَلَمْ يُبْدِها لَهُمْ — **"But Joseph kept it to himself and did not disclose it to them."** Just as His saying: "and did not disclose it to them" is an explanatory conjunction for His saying: "But Joseph kept it to himself."

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: فَأَسَرَّها — **"But he kept it to himself,"** i.e., he hid this word they said; meaning he did not address what they attributed to him of theft, nor did he deny it, nor did he explain the reality of the situation. Rather, "Joseph kept it to himself and did not disclose it to them." It was as if someone were saying: "How did he keep it to himself?" and the answer was that he قالَ — **"said"** [inwardly]: أَنْتُمْ شَرٌّ مَكاناً — **"You are in a worse plight"** and in a worse state because of the contradiction in your words, the manifest instinct of envy in your souls, and your audacity to lie in the presence of the Vizier after all this honor and kindness. وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما تَصِفُونَ — **"and Allah knows best what you allege"**—that a brother of his had stolen before—so he did not call them liars in their description nor did he deny it.

Some commentators mentioned that the meaning of his saying: "You are in a worse plight" etc., is: "You are in a worse state than him because you stole your brother from your father, and Allah knows best whether a brother of his stole before or not."

To this it may be said: it is possible that this meaning was part of what Joseph intended by his saying: "You are in a worse plight," but the discourse is about what his brothers received from this saying of his in this specific context where they did not recognize Joseph (عليه‌السلام) and he did not want to make himself known to them. His saying in such a context only applies as previously mentioned.

Perhaps some mentioned that what Joseph kept to himself and did not disclose to them was his word: "You are in a worse plight," so he did not address them with it, then he spoke aloud saying: "and Allah knows best what you allege." This is far-fetched and not understood from the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالُوا يا أَيُّهَا الْعَزِيزُ إِنَّ لَهُ أَباً شَيْخاً كَبِيراً فَخُذْ أَحَدَنا مَكانَهُ إِنَّا نَراكَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — **"They said, ‘O Vizier! He has a very elderly father. So take one of us in his place. Indeed we see you to be a virtuous man.’"** (12:78). The context of the verses indicates that they only said this when they saw that he deserved to be taken and enslaved. They mentioned that they had given their father a pledge before Allah to return him to him, so it was not in their power to return to their father without him being with them. At that point, they resolved to ransom him with one of them if the Vizier would accept, and they spoke to the Vizier about that—to take whichever of them he wished and release their accused brother so they could return him to his father.

The meaning of the verse is clear. In the phrasing, there is softening, an appeal for mercy, and an arousal of the attribute of chivalry and kindness in the Vizier.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالَ مَعاذَ اللهِ أَنْ نَأْخُذَ إِلَّا مَنْ وَجَدْنا مَتاعَنا عِنْدَهُ إِنَّا إِذاً لَظالِمُونَ — **"He said, ‘Allah forbid that we should take anyone except him with whom we found our goods, for then we would indeed be wrongdoers.’"** (12:79). This was a rejection from him (عليه‌السلام) of their request to take one of them in his place. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَلَمَّا اسْتَيْأَسُوا مِنْهُ خَلَصُوا نَجِيًّا — **"When they had despaired of [moving] him, they withdrew for a private conference..."** (12:80) until the end of the verse. It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "**Despair** (اليأس) is the severing of hope in a matter... and *istay’asa* is like *ya’isa* in meaning... and the **confidant** (النجي) refers to the people who confer privately (يتناجون); it is the same for singular and plural. The Exalted said: وَقَرَّبْناهُ نَجِيًّا — **"and We brought him near for a private conference"** (19:52). This is permissible because it is a verbal noun used as an attribute. Private conference (المناجاة) is whispering, and its root is from *najwah*, which is an elevated part of the earth, for it is as if each one raises the secret to his companion in concealment. *Najwa* can be a noun or a verbal noun; the Exalted said: وَإِذْ هُمْ نَجْوى — **"and when they confer privately"** (17:47), and He said regarding the verbal noun: إِنَّمَا النَّجْوى مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ — **"Private conference is only from Satan"** (58:10). And he said: 'The man departed (برح) with a departure (براحا)' if he moved away from his place."

The pronoun in His saying: فَلَمَّا اسْتَيْأَسُوا مِنْهُ — **"When they had despaired of him"** refers to Joseph. It is also possible that it refers to his brother. The meaning is: "When they"—i.e., Joseph’s brothers—"despaired of him"—i.e., of Joseph releasing their brother, even by taking one of them in his stead—خَلَصُوا — **"they withdrew"** and went out from among the people to a vacant space نَجِيًّا — **"for a private conference,"** conferring about their affair: should they return to their father when he had taken from them a pledge before Allah to return their brother to him, or should they stay there, though there was no benefit in their staying? What should they do?

قالَ كَبِيرُهُمْ — **"Their eldest said,"** addressing the rest of them: أَلَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ أَباكُمْ قَدْ أَخَذَ عَلَيْكُمْ مَوْثِقاً مِنَ اللهِ — **"Do you not know that your father has taken a pledge from you before Allah"** that you would not return to him from this journey of yours except with your brother, وَمِنْ قَبْلُ — **"and before"** this incident ما فَرَّطْتُمْ — **"how you failed"**—i.e., your failure and negligence—فِي — **"in"** the matter of يُوسُفَ — **"Joseph."** You promised your father that you would protect him and return him to him safely, but you threw him into the pit, then sold him to the caravan, then informed your father that the wolf had eaten him.

فَلَنْ أَبْرَحَ الْأَرْضَ — **"So I will never leave the land,"** i.e., since the situation is as it is, I will not move away nor depart from the land of Egypt حَتَّى يَأْذَنَ لِي أَبِي — **"until my father permits me"** by releasing his hand from the pledge I gave him أَوْ يَحْكُمَ اللهُ لِي وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْحاكِمِينَ — **"or Allah judges for me, and He is the best of judges"** by making for me a way to escape from this narrowness that has closed every door to me—and that is either by the release of my brother from the Vizier’s hand through a way I do not expect, or by my death, or by some other path!!

"As for me, I choose to remain here. As for you, return to your father..." until the end of what is mentioned in the following two verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ارْجِعُوا إِلى أَبِيكُمْ فَقُولُوا يا أَبانا إِنَّ ابْنَكَ سَرَقَ وَما شَهِدْنا إِلَّا بِما عَلِمْنا وَما كُنَّا لِلْغَيْبِ حافِظِينَ — **"Return to your father and say, ‘O father! Your son has indeed committed theft. We do not testify except to what we know, and we were not guardians of the Unseen.’"** (12:81). It has been said that the meaning of His saying: وَما شَهِدْنا إِلَّا بِما عَلِمْنا — **"We do not testify except to what we know"** is: "We did not testify in this testimony of ours—'Your son has indeed committed theft'—except to what we knew of his theft." It has also been said the meaning is: "We did not testify before the Vizier that the thief is taken for his theft and enslaved except to what we knew of the judgment of the issue." It is said they only said that when Jacob said to them: "How did the man know that the thief is taken for his theft and enslaved? He only knew that through your words." The first of the two meanings is closer to the context.

His saying: وَما كُنَّا لِلْغَيْبِ حافِظِينَ — **"and we were not guardians of the Unseen"**: it has been said this means: "We did not know that your son would steal and thus be taken and enslaved; we were only relying on the outward state of affairs. Had we known that, we would not have hastened to take him with us nor would we have offered the pledge."

The truth is that the "Unseen" (الغيب) refers to his being a thief while they were ignorant of it. The meaning of the verse is: "Your son stole, and we did not testify regarding the penalty of theft except to what we knew, and we did not know that he had stolen the drinking-cup and that he would be taken for it so that we might have refrained from that testimony; we did not think that of him."

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَسْئَلِ الْقَرْيَةَ الَّتِي كُنَّا فِيها وَالْعِيرَ الَّتِي أَقْبَلْنا فِيها وَإِنَّا لَصادِقُونَ — **"Ask the [people of the] town where we were, and the caravan with which we came. We are indeed telling the truth.’"** (12:82). That is: "Ask all those who accompanied us in this journey or witnessed the course of our state with the Vizier, so that not the slightest doubt remains with you that we did not fail in his matter, but rather that he stole and was enslaved."

The "town" (القرية) where they were refers to the city of Egypt—apparently—and the "caravan" (العير) with which they came refers to the group they were in, whose men accompanied them in going to Egypt and returning from it, and then they came accompanying them. Therefore, they followed the offer of the question with their saying: وَإِنَّا لَصادِقُونَ — **"We are indeed telling the truth,"** i.e., in what we inform you of his theft and his enslavement for it, and we charge you with the question to remove the doubt from your soul.
